title: Insulin quiet hours
body: Research suggests that longer gaps between meals may give insulin signaling more time to settle, which some people find helpful for steady energy (individual responses vary).

title: Fat burning isn’t a light switch
body: Your body is always using a mix of fuels. Fasting windows may shift that mix over time, but there is no single moment when “fat burning turns on.”

title: Focus and distraction
body: Some people report clearer focus during short fasts. Others feel foggy. Hydration, sleep, and caffeine timing often matter as much as the fast itself.

title: Autophagy buzzword
body: Autophagy is a normal cellular cleanup process. It’s exciting science, but human evidence tying specific fasting lengths to measurable autophagy outcomes is still developing.

title: One size does not fit all
body: Fasting can be fine for some people and unwise for others (for example with certain medications, pregnancy, diabetes, or a history of eating disorders). When in doubt, ask a clinician.

title: Calories still count
body: Time-restricted eating changes when you eat, not whether energy balance matters. Overall patterns across the week still drive most weight change.

title: Hydration and headaches
body: Many fasting headaches are dehydration or caffeine withdrawal. Water and electrolytes (if appropriate for you) can be the simplest fix.

title: Consistency beats perfection
body: A sustainable schedule you can keep for months often teaches you more about your body than an extreme plan you abandon after a week.

title: Break the fast gently
body: After a long fast, many people feel better starting with a smaller, easy-to-digest meal rather than a huge celebratory feast.

title: Muscle maintenance
body: Protein and resistance training across the week may matter more for muscle than any single fasting window.

title: Sleep is part of the stack
body: Short sleep can mimic “fasting brain fog.” If your fast makes sleep worse, it may be worth adjusting timing or length.

title: Not a medical treatment
body: This app shares general education, not medical advice. Use it as a tracker and conversation starter with professionals you trust.
